concerns.text: The plan mandates a 'new element' constraint (FR-010) where test folds cannot contain elements unseen in training. However, the validation strategy is Leave-One-System-Out (LOSO). In a binary alloy dataset, if a system (e.g., Cu-Zn) is held out, both Cu and Zn are removed from the training set. This makes the 'new element' check trivially true for every fold (the test set *always* contains new elements relative to the training set). The plan conflates 'new system' (valid LOSO) with 'new element' (impossible under strict LOSO for binaries). This renders the validation logic contradictory: either the model is tested on elements it has never seen (violating FR-010 as written) or the LOSO strategy is not strictly applied (violating FR-003).

The plan proposes a power analysis (FR-011) on the total sample size to justify the study. However, the primary validation metric is LOSO cross-validation performance (MAE/R² on held-out systems). Standard power analysis (e.g., t-test based) assumes independent samples. In LOSO, the 'samples' are systems, not individual data points. Calculating power based on the total number of data points (N ~ 50-200) rather than the number of systems (K ~ 10-20) will grossly overestimate statistical power, leading to a false 'PASS' on FR-014 even if the number of held-out systems is too small to generalize. The power analysis methodology is mismatched to the LOSO validation regime.

The plan compares the Random Forest model against a 'Null Model (predicting global mean temperature)' and requires a statistically significant reduction in MAE (p < 0.05). However, the null model is trained on the *entire* dataset (or the training fold), while the RF is trained on the same data. If the RF model is overfitting or if the features have any correlation with temperature (which they must, by hypothesis), the RF will almost trivially outperform the global mean. The statistical test (paired t-test) on fold-level errors may be invalid if the errors are not independent (e.g., if one system dominates the error distribution). More critically, the 'significance' of beating a global mean is a weak scientific claim for phase diagrams, where the null hypothesis should be 'no predictive power beyond random noise' or 'performance equal to a simple linear interpolation', not just 'better than the average'.
